title: CI: default to sqlite
body:
Followup to #20318: now that sqlite is the podman default,
enforce that in CI as well. Test boltdb only in Prior Fedora.

In the process, discovered & cleaned up some duplication
and unused YAML anchors.

Signed-off-by: Ed Santiago <santiago@redhat.com>
```release-note
None
```

comments.body:
Maybe it's this????? https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/1ef61cf99cd4019b6a6afc2efeaf10223100fa3b/contrib/cirrus/lib.sh#L293-L294

This runs installed-podman, which is old-podman, i.e. boltdb-podman, and by virtue of simply having been run, blam, we are forever stuck with boltdb?

Giving up for today.
This was trickier than I'd expected, but I consider it ready for review.

[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *cevich*, *edsantiago* The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Fpodman). The pull request process is described [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process)
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - ~~[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/OWNERS)~~ [edsantiago] Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment
Moving back to WIP as I test a (possibly) cleaner solution to the containers.conf question.

Idea (since this is WIP again): I never liked the podman call in `remove_packaged_podman_files()`.  I don't think that socket path will ever change, maybe it could simply be hard-coded, or use the result of a `find -name podman.socket` or somesuch?

Another idea: Use a `podman system reset --force` to clear the setup before removing the binary.

>maybe it could simply be hard-coded

That's exactly what I did in this push :-). No luck. Now trying to figure out why.

And, back to ready. I Am not going to mess with this any more.

Current working hypothesis. Something in `automation_images` is running podman (it's too difficult for me to tell), so the images are already tainted by the time we get them in podman CI. It might be possible for someone with `get_ci_vm` access to figure this out. I can't run that, but I did try `Rerun in Terminal`, with inconclusive results.

Bummer and interesting.  I wonder if maybe it's happening on boot via `podman.socket` and/or `podman.service`.  If re-run in terminal didn't help, get_ci_vm probably wouldn't either. Anyway, I'm assuming you also had no luck with my `podman system reset --force` idea.  Oh well.   It was probably worth trying.

Happy green test buttons.  I'm going to merge this now to save the remaining pieces of @edsantiago 's sanity.  We can adjust as needed later.

/lgtm
@edsantiago this apparently still has a do-not-merge/hold label.  Safe to remove at this point?
Looks like the hold was from Dan's review.  I think it's safe to just merge it @TomSweeneyRedHat

{eyes peaking between almost closed fingers over face} :eyes: 
/hold cancel
